title: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease
items.title: Noninvasive Assessment in Liver Disease
items.content: The evaluation of liver disease has evolved to prioritize noninvasive methods for assessing fibrosis stage over traditional liver biopsies. This shift allows clinicians to potentially avoid invasive procedures while still obtaining critical information about disease activity and progression.
keyPoints: Liver biopsy is the criterion standard but less needed now for diagnosis, more for grading (activity) and staging (fibrosis).
Noninvasive methods are increasingly helpful in assessing fibrosis stage.
These noninvasive means may allow avoidance of biopsy in many patients.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease
items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Liver Disease
items.content: An accurate diagnosis of liver disease can typically be achieved through a comprehensive patient history, physical examination, and selected laboratory tests. Radiologic examinations may also play a crucial role in certain cases by providing diagnostic insights.
keyPoints: Diagnosis usually made with patient's history, physical exam, and lab tests.
Radiologic exams are helpful or diagnostic in some circumstances.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease

items.title: Dual Blood Supply of the Liver
items.content: The liver receives blood from two distinct sources: approximately 20% from the hepatic artery, which is oxygen-rich, and about 80% from the portal vein, which carries nutrient-rich blood. This dual supply is crucial for its metabolic functions.
keyPoints: Liver receives ~20% of blood flow from the hepatic artery (oxygen-rich).
Approximately 80% of liver's blood supply comes from the portal vein (nutrient-rich) originating from the stomach, intestines, pancreas, and spleen.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.title: Functional Organization of Hepatic Acinus
items.content: The liver is functionally organized into acini rather than lobules. Blood enters the acinus from portal areas (zone 1) and flows through sinusoids to terminal hepatic veins (zone 3), with hepatocytes in zone 2 facilitating various metabolic processes.
keyPoints: Liver's functional unit is the acinus, not the lobule.
Blood flow: Zone 1 (portal areas) → Zone 2 (hepatocytes) → Zone 3 (terminal hepatic veins).
Bile flows in a countercurrent pattern from zone 3 to zone 1.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.title: Hepatocyte Roles and Liver Function Tests
items.content: Hepatocytes perform essential functions such as synthesizing serum proteins, producing bile, regulating nutrients, and metabolizing compounds. Common liver function tests include serum bilirubin, albumin levels, and prothrombin time to assess these activities.
keyPoints: Hepatocytes synthesize essential serum proteins (e.g., albumin, coagulation factors).
Liver produces bile and regulates nutrients like glucose and lipids.
Common liver function tests: Serum bilirubin, serum albumin, prothrombin time.
Abnormalities in these tests indicate hepatic dysfunction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.title: Classifying Liver Diseases by Injury Pattern
items.content: Liver diseases are classified into hepatocellular, cholestatic (obstructive), or mixed patterns based on the predominant features of liver injury. Hepatocellular diseases involve liver injury and inflammation, while cholestatic diseases primarily inhibit bile flow. Mixed pattern diseases exhibit both hepatocellular and cholestatic injuries.
keyPoints: Hepatocellular diseases: e.g., viral hepatitis, alcoholic liver disease
Cholestatic diseases: e.g., gallstone obstruction, primary biliary cholangitis
Mixed pattern diseases: e.g., cholestatic forms of viral hepatitis
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER DISEASES

items.title: Approach to Evaluating Liver Disease
items.content: Evaluation of liver disease involves determining the etiologic diagnosis, grading the severity (activity), and staging the progression. Diagnosis considers the pattern of injury and specific causes. Grading assesses whether the disease is active or inactive and its severity level, while staging estimates the stage in the natural history from early to end-stage.
keyPoints: Evaluation focuses on etiologic diagnosis, grading (severity/activity), and staging (course progression)
Grading: mild, moderate, severe; active or inactive
Staging: precirrhotic, cirrhotic, end-stage
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER DISEASES

items.title: Fatigue in Liver Disease: Nature and Timing
items.content: Fatigue is the most characteristic symptom of liver disease, often described as lethargy or weakness. It typically arises after activity rather than being present after rest, indicating 'afternoon' fatigue.
keyPoints: Fatigue is the most common and characteristic symptom of liver disease.
It is usually intermittent and variable in severity.
Occurs after activity/exercise, not severe after adequate rest.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Jaundice as a Marker of Liver Disease Severity
items.content: Jaundice is the hallmark symptom and reliable marker of liver disease severity. It typically becomes noticeable with bilirubin levels above 43 μmol/L (2.5 mg/dL).
keyPoints: Jaundice is a key indicator of liver disease severity.
Bilirubin level <43 μmol/L (2.5 mg/dL) usually means jaundice is not detectable.
Dark urine often precedes noticeable scleral icterus.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Risk Factors for Liver Disease
items.content: Key risk factors include alcohol use, medication history, personal habits, sexual activity, and exposure to high-risk individuals. A detailed history should assess these areas.
keyPoints: Important risk factors: alcohol use, medications, personal habits, sexual activity, travel, exposure risks.
Assessing sexual activity is crucial for viral hepatitis risk.
Family history of liver disease is significant.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Assessing Risk Factors for Viral Hepatitis
items.content: Understanding a patient's history of injection drug use is crucial in evaluating the risk for hepatitis B, C, and D. Injection drug use remains the most common risk factor for hepatitis C.
keyPoints: Injection drug use is the single most common risk factor for hepatitis C.
Blood transfusions before 1992 are a significant risk factor for chronic hepatitis C.
Transfusions before 1986 pose a risk for hepatitis B.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.title: Evaluating Alcohol Intake in Liver Disease
items.content: A detailed history of alcohol intake is essential to determine its role in liver disease and guide management. The CAGE questionnaire aids in diagnosing alcohol dependence or abuse.
keyPoints: 70% of adults in the U.S. consume alcohol, but significant intake (>2 drinks/day for women, >3 for men) is less common.
Alcoholism is defined by behavioral patterns and consequences rather than quantity.
The CAGE questionnaire helps diagnose alcohol dependence and abuse.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.title: Family History in Liver Disease Assessment
items.content: A family history can indicate genetic liver diseases such as Wilson disease, hemochromatosis, or alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. Specific symptoms and family histories guide further investigation.
keyPoints: Wilson disease should be investigated if there's early severe liver disease with a relevant family history.
Hemochromatosis is suggested by cirrhosis, diabetes, or endocrine failure in adulthood.
Abnormal iron studies prompt HFE gene genotyping for C282Y and H63D mutations.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.title: Identifying Early Signs of Hepatic Decompensation
items.content: The physical examination can reveal early signs of hepatic decompensation, such as icterus and spider angiomata. These findings, while not specific to one diagnosis, are crucial in guiding further diagnostic evaluation.
keyPoints: Icterus is detectable when serum bilirubin level is ≥43 μmol/L (2.5 mg/dL).
Spider angiomata fill from the center outward and occur on arms, face, and upper torso.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.title: Assessing Hepatic Encephalopathy in Liver Failure
items.content: Hepatic encephalopathy is a critical sign of hepatic failure, characterized by changes in mental status and specific physical findings. Early recognition can guide urgent management.
keyPoints: Signs include asterixis, confusion, stupor, and coma.
Fetor hepaticus indicates liver failure with portal-venous shunting.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.title: Evaluating Splenomegaly in Chronic Liver Disease
items.content: Splenomegaly is a subtle but significant finding in chronic liver disease, suggesting underlying cirrhosis. Ultrasound can confirm this physical finding.
keyPoints: Splenomegaly suggests underlying cirrhosis.
Ultrasound confirms splenomegaly when physical examination is equivocal.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.title: Assessing Hepatic Encephalopathy with Trail-Making Test
items.content: The trail-making test is a useful tool for assessing hepatic encephalopathy. It involves connecting numbered circles as quickly as possible, with normal completion times ranging from 15 to 30 seconds. Prolonged times suggest early hepatic encephalopathy.
keyPoints: Trail-making test normal range: 15–30 s
Prolonged time indicates early hepatic encephalopathy
Useful for mental status examination in liver disease
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.title: Identifying Hepatopulmonary Syndrome through Oxygen Saturation
items.content: Hepatopulmonary syndrome, characterized by hypoxemia and pulmonary arteriovenous shunting in patients with liver disease, can be screened using pulse oximetry. This condition is marked by platypsia and orthodeoxia, where oxygen desaturation occurs upon standing.
keyPoints: Hepatopulmonary syndrome: liver disease + hypoxemia + pulmonary arteriovenous shunting
Characterized by platypsia and orthodeoxia
Pulse oximetry is a reliable screening test
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.title: Common Causes of Acute Liver Disease
items.content: The most common causes of acute liver disease include viral hepatitis (particularly hepatitis A, B, and C), drug-induced liver injury, cholangitis, and alcohol-related liver disease. Liver biopsy is generally not required unless the diagnosis remains unclear after thorough clinical and laboratory investigation.
keyPoints: Common causes: viral hepatitis (A, B, C), drug-induced liver injury, cholangitis, alcohol-related liver disease
Liver biopsy usually unnecessary except when diagnosis is unclear
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Laboratory Testing in Liver Disease Diagnosis
items.content: Initial assessment of liver disease involves measuring serum alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (AlkP), direct and total bilirubin, albumin, and prothrombin time. These tests help differentiate between hepatocellular versus cholestatic liver disease and assess if the condition is acute or chronic.
keyPoints: Initial blood tests: ALT, AST, AlkP, bilirubin, albumin, prothrombin time
Tests indicate hepatocellular vs. cholestatic disease; help determine acuteness/chronicity
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Imaging in Liver Disease
items.content: US, CT, and MRI are primary imaging modalities for liver disease. US and CT are sensitive for detecting biliary duct dilation, while MRCP is preferred over ERCP for visualizing the biliary tree without risks like pancreatitis.
keyPoints: Primary imaging: US, CT, MRI
US and CT detect biliary duct dilation; MRCP preferred for biliary visualization without risk of pancreatitis
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Imaging Modalities for Hepatic Lesions
items.content: Multidetector or spiral CT and MRI with contrast enhancement are preferred for identifying hepatic masses, staging liver tumors, and preoperative assessment. Contrast-enhanced US is emerging as a valuable tool due to its real-time assessment capabilities without nephrotoxicity or radiation risks.
keyPoints: CT and MRI are the procedures of choice for hepatic mass identification and evaluation.
Sensitivity of hepatic imaging is increasing, but specificity remains problematic.
Contrast-enhanced US offers advantages like no nephrotoxicity or radiation exposure.
Limitations of contrast-enhanced US include patient body habitus and operator skill.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Role of Liver Biopsy in Chronic Liver Disease
items.content: Liver biopsy is the gold standard for evaluating chronic liver disease, essential for diagnosing specific conditions and assessing fibrosis severity. A sample length of 1.5–2 cm with 10 portal tracts ensures reliable assessment.
keyPoints: Biopsy remains crucial for diagnosis and staging in chronic liver disease.
Sample size of 1.5–2 cm with 10 portal tracts is necessary for accurate fibrosis assessment.
Biopsy should be used when it materially affects management decisions due to its invasive nature.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.title: Why Liver Biopsy is Essential for Grading and Staging Chronic Liver Disease?
items.content: Liver biopsy remains the most accurate method to assess both the severity (grading) and stage of liver disease, particularly in chronic conditions. While serum aminotransferase levels are convenient markers, they often fail to accurately reflect the true extent of disease activity or severity due to their variability.
keyPoints: Liver biopsy is the gold standard for assessing both grading and staging of liver disease.
Serum aminotransferase levels do not reliably indicate disease severity; normal levels can occur even with moderate chronic hepatitis B or C.
Histologic scales such as METAVIR (0 to 4+) and Ishak (0 to 6+) are commonly used for staging fibrosis in liver biopsies.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.title: How Noninvasive Tests Fall Short in Early Fibrosis Detection?
items.content: Noninvasive tests, including blood markers and imaging techniques like elastography, are not sufficiently reliable for detecting early stages of liver fibrosis. These methods often become abnormal only at middle to late stages of cirrhosis.
keyPoints: Noninvasive tests can suggest advanced fibrosis but are unreliable for early detection.
Markers such as mild elevations in bilirubin, prolonged prothrombin time, and slight decreases in serum albumin indicate worsening fibrosis.
Elastography measures liver stiffness rather than direct fibrosis and is influenced by inflammation and other factors.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.title: Why Staging Liver Disease Matters for Prognosis and Management?
items.content: Staging liver disease, particularly identifying cirrhosis, is crucial for determining prognosis, recommending therapy, and managing complications. Patients with cirrhosis require specific screenings to prevent further health issues.
keyPoints: The degree of fibrosis (staged 0 to 4+ on METAVIR or 0 to 6+ on Ishak) is critical for prognosis and management decisions.
Cirrhotic patients need screening for esophageal varices and hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC).
Staging helps in deciding the necessity of therapy and surveillance measures.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease
items.question: Which of the following is considered the criterion standard in evaluating liver disease?
items.explanation: Liver biopsy is traditionally regarded as the gold standard for evaluating liver disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease
items.question: What has become increasingly helpful in assessing fibrosis stage, potentially reducing the need for liver biopsies?
items.explanation: Noninvasive methods have become more useful in assessing fibrosis, possibly avoiding the need for biopsy.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease
items.question: What is less frequently needed now for diagnosing liver disease compared to grading and staging?
items.explanation: Liver biopsy, while still important for grading and staging, is less frequently needed solely for diagnosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease

items.question: What percentage of the liver's blood supply is derived from the hepatic artery?
items.explanation: The liver receives ~20% of its blood flow from the oxygen-rich hepatic artery.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.question: Which cell type constitutes two-thirds of the liver's mass?
items.explanation: The majority of cells in the liver are hepatocytes, which constitute two-thirds of its mass.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.question: Where do Kupffer cells primarily reside within the liver?
items.explanation: Kupffer cells usually lie within the sinusoidal vascular space.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.question: Which of the following is a measure of hepatic conjugation and excretion?
items.explanation: The serum bilirubin level is a measure of hepatic conjugation and excretion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.question: Which pattern of liver disease is characterized by features of inhibition of bile flow?
items.explanation: In cholestatic diseases, such as gallstone or malignant obstruction, features of inhibition of bile flow predominate.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER DISEASES

items.question: What is the primary focus when diagnosing liver disease?
options: Pattern of disease presentation and specific etiologic diagnosis
Symptoms such as jaundice and fatigue
items.explanation: Diagnosis should focus on the pattern of disease presentation (hepatocellular, cholestatic, or mixed injury) as well as on the specific etiologic diagnosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER DISEASES

items.question: What does 'staging' refer to in the evaluation of liver disease?
options: Assessment of severity or activity of disease
Estimation of the point in the course of the natural history of the disease
items.explanation: Staging refers to estimation of the point in the course of the natural history of the disease, whether early or late; or precirrhotic, cirrhotic, or end-stage.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER DISEASES

items.question: Which symptom is most characteristic of liver disease?
items.explanation: Fatigue is described as the most common and most characteristic symptom of liver disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: What typically causes right-upper-quadrant discomfort or ache in liver diseases?
options: Stretching or irritation of Glisson's capsule
items.explanation: Right-upper-quadrant discomfort or ache arises from stretching or irritation of Glisson's capsule.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: What is a common presenting symptom in cholestatic forms of chronic liver disease?
items.explanation: In cholestatic forms such as primary biliary cholangitis and sclerosing cholangitis, itching is often the presenting symptom.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following conditions is associated with indirect (unconjugated) hyperbilirubinemia?
items.explanation: Jaundice without dark urine usually indicates indirect (unconjugated) hyperbilirubinemia, typical of hemolytic anemia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: Which factor is the single most common risk factor for hepatitis C?
options: Travel to a developing area of the world
items.explanation: Injection drug use is identified as the single most common risk factor for hepatitis C.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.question: What was an important risk factor for chronic hepatitis C before 1992?
options: Travel to a developing area of the world
items.explanation: Before 1992, blood transfusions were an important risk factor for chronic hepatitis C due to lack of sensitive enzyme immunoassays.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.question: Which type of hepatitis is uncommon in developed nations but common in Asia and Africa?
items.explanation: Hepatitis E is more common in Asia and Africa but uncommon in developed nations.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.question: What daily alcohol intake level is associated with an increased rate of alcoholic liver disease in men?
items.explanation: An intake of three drinks (33–45 g) per day is associated with an increased rate of alcoholic liver disease in men.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.question: Which physical finding is considered the most reliable in liver examination?
items.explanation: Hepatic tenderness is noted as the most reliable physical finding in liver examination.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: In which condition might jaundice be detectable even if serum bilirubin levels are below 43 μmol/L?
options: In the presence of hepatic encephalopathy
items.explanation: Jaundice may remain detectable below this level during recovery due to protein and tissue binding of conjugated bilirubin.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: What is the best method for appreciating icterus in fair-skinned individuals?
options: Examining mucous membranes below the tongue
Checking skin coloration under natural light
items.explanation: Icterus is best appreciated when the sclera is inspected under natural light in fair-skinned individuals.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following signs can be difficult to detect in dark-skinned individuals?
items.explanation: Spider angiomata can be difficult to detect in dark-skinned individuals.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.question: Which test is used to help measure hepatic encephalopathy by having the patient connect numbered circles as rapidly as possible?
items.explanation: The trail-making test is used to measure hepatic encephalopathy by assessing the time taken to connect numbered circles.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is characterized by collateral veins radiating from the umbilicus due to recanulation of the umbilical vein?
items.explanation: Caput medusa is characterized by collateral veins radiating from the umbilicus due to recanulation of the umbilical vein.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: What skin condition in liver disease is caused by high serum levels of lipids and cholesterol?
items.explanation: Xanthelasma occurs in liver disease due to high serum levels of lipids and cholesterol.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common cause of acute liver disease?
items.explanation: Hemochromatosis is more commonly associated with chronic liver disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: In the diagnosis of acute liver disease, when is a liver biopsy typically NOT needed?
options: When drug-induced liver injury is considered
When the diagnosis remains unclear despite thorough investigation
When alcohol-related liver disease is suspected
items.explanation: Liver biopsy usually isn't needed unless the diagnosis remains unclear after clinical and laboratory evaluation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging modality is most commonly used as a first-line option for suspected obstructive jaundice?
items.explanation: Ultrasound and CT are first-line options for investigating suspected obstructive jaundice.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which test is used to define whether alkaline phosphatase elevations are due to liver disease?
items.explanation: $\gamma$-glutamyl transpeptidase (γGT) helps define whether alkaline phosphatase elevations are due to liver disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging modality is recommended for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) screening?
items.explanation: Ultrasound (US) is the recommended modality for HCC screening.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is a major advantage of contrast-enhanced US over CT or MRI in hepatic imaging?
options: No risk of nephrotoxicity and radiation exposure
items.explanation: Contrast-enhanced US allows real-time assessment without the risks of nephrotoxicity and radiation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is necessary for an accurate assessment of fibrosis in a liver biopsy?
options: A length of 1.0–1.5 cm with 5 portal tracts
A length of 1.5–2 cm with 10 portal tracts
A length of 2.0–2.5 cm with 15 portal tracts
Any size with at least one portal tract
items.explanation: An accurate assessment requires a biopsy sample of 1.5–2 cm with 10 portal tracts.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.question: Which method is considered the most accurate for assessing the severity and stage of liver disease?
items.explanation: Liver biopsy is the most accurate means of assessing both severity and stage of liver disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.question: In chronic hepatitis B, normal serum aminotransferase levels may indicate which of the following?
options: Inactive carrier state or mild chronic hepatitis B
Severe chronic hepatitis B with high disease activity
items.explanation: Normal serum aminotransferase levels in patients with hepatitis B surface antigen may indicate the inactive carrier state or reflect mild chronic hepatitis B.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically used to assess advanced fibrosis noninvasively?
items.explanation: Liver biopsy is not a noninvasive test; it's used to assess fibrosis directly.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.question: What limitation do noninvasive markers for liver disease have?
options: They are always accurate in reflecting disease activity.
They can be affected by disease activity and inflammation.
They provide a definitive diagnosis of cirrhosis.
They are unaffected by hepatocyte necrosis.
items.explanation: Noninvasive markers for liver disease can be affected by disease activity, limiting their reliability.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease
items.statement: Radiologic examinations are always necessary for diagnosing liver disease.
items.explanation: Radiologic exams can be helpful or diagnostic in some cases, but they are not always required.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease
items.statement: Clinical history and physical examination alone can often lead to an accurate diagnosis of liver disease.
items.explanation: A careful patient history and physical exam, along with a few lab tests, can usually accurately diagnose liver disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease

items.statement: Hepatocytes exhibit endocytotic activity on their apical pole.
items.explanation: Endocytotic and pinocytotic activities occur at the basolateral side of hepatocytes.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.statement: The liver is organized into lobules for functional purposes.
items.explanation: Functionally, the liver is organized into acini rather than lobules.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.statement: Jaundice is a typical presenting symptom of liver disease.
items.explanation: Typical presenting symptoms of liver disease include jaundice.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER DISEASES

items.statement: Liver disease can only be diagnosed in symptomatic patients.
items.explanation: Many patients are diagnosed with liver disease who have no symptoms and abnormalities found through routine tests.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER DISEASES

items.statement: Itching is a common symptom in all forms of liver disease.
items.explanation: Itching can occur in any liver disease, particularly once cirrhosis develops.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.statement: Jaundice is detectable with a bilirubin level <43 μmol/L.
items.explanation: Jaundice is rarely detectable with a bilirubin level <43 μmol/L.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.statement: Tattooing and body piercing are major risk factors for hepatitis B, C, and D.
items.explanation: Tattooing and body piercing are mentioned as exposures but rarely lead to infection.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.statement: Hepatitis E can become chronic in immunosuppressed individuals.
items.explanation: In immunosuppressed individuals, hepatitis E can present as a chronic infection.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.statement: Hepatomegaly is a very reliable sign of liver disease.
items.explanation: Hepatomegaly is not considered very reliable due to variability in the liver's size and shape.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.statement: Signs of hepatic encephalopathy include asterixis and flapping tremors.
items.explanation: Asterixis and flapping tremors are physical findings associated with hepatic encephalopathy.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.statement: Hepatopulmonary syndrome is characterized by platypsia and orthodeoxia.
items.explanation: Hepatopulmonary syndrome involves shortness of breath and oxygen desaturation upon assuming an upright position, known as platypsia and orthodeoxia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.statement: Kayser-Fleischer rings are indicative of primary biliary cholangitis.
items.explanation: Kayser-Fleischer rings occur in Wilson disease, not primary biliary cholangitis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 2)

items.statement: Hepatitis E virus is a common cause of chronic hepatitis.
items.explanation: Hepatitis E virus is a rare cause of chronic hepatitis, mostly in immunosuppressed or immunodeficient persons.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: ERCP carries no risk of pancreatitis.
items.explanation: ERCP does carry a risk of pancreatitis, unlike MRCP.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: Magnetic resonance elastography is less expensive than US elastography.
items.explanation: Magnetic resonance elastography is more sensitive but also more expensive than US elastography.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 2)

items.statement: Liver biopsy should be used only when it will materially contribute to management decisions.
items.explanation: Due to its invasive nature and potential complications, liver biopsy is reserved for cases where it significantly impacts management.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.statement: Serum aminotransferase levels are reliable indicators of the severity of chronic liver diseases.
items.explanation: Serum aminotransferase levels do not always reliably reflect disease severity.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.statement: Elastography measures fibrosis directly and is unaffected by inflammation.
items.explanation: Elastography measures liver stiffness, not fibrosis per se, and can be affected by inflammation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE

items.assertion: The sinusoids in the liver allow free flow of plasma but not cellular elements.
items.reason: Sinusoidal endothelial cells have prominent fenestrae that permit this selective permeability.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.assertion: Activated stellate cells produce collagen and matrix in the liver.
items.reason: Stellate cells are located within the sinusoids of the liver.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why activated stellate cells produce collagen.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.assertion: Grading in the evaluation of liver disease refers to the estimation of the point in the course of the natural history of the disease.
items.reason: Grading refers to assessment of the severity or activity of disease—active or inactive as well as mild, moderate, or severe.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because grading assesses severity or activity, not the point in the course of the disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · LIVER DISEASES

items.assertion: Fatigue in liver disease is usually present after adequate rest.
items.reason: Fatigue typically arises after activity or exercise and is rarely severe after adequate rest.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; fatigue in liver disease is not usually present after adequate rest.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: Sexual exposure is a common mode of spread for hepatitis C.
items.reason: Sexual exposure is a common mode of spread of hepatitis B and D but is uncommon for hepatitis C.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; sexual exposure is uncommon for hepatitis C.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: A family history of emphysema should lead to investigation of $ α1 $ antitrypsin levels.
items.reason: $ α1 $ antitrypsin deficiency is associated with liver disease and emphysema.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why a family history of emphysema should prompt investigation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.assertion: Alcoholism is defined by the amount of alcohol consumed.
items.reason: Alcohol abuse is characterized by repetitive drinking patterns with adverse effects.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; alcoholism is defined by behavioral patterns and consequences, not just the amount.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · CLINICAL HISTORY (part 2)

items.assertion: Splenomegaly suggests underlying cirrhosis in chronic liver disease.
items.reason: Ultrasound methods allow confirmation of splenomegaly, supporting its significance as a physical finding.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason supports the assertion about the significance of splenomegaly.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.assertion: The first signs of hepatic encephalopathy include confusion and disorientation.
items.reason: Hepatic encephalopathy begins with subtle changes such as altered sleep patterns and irritability.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain the assertion about initial signs.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION (part 1)

items.assertion: Liver biopsy is important in diagnosing autoimmune hepatitis.
items.reason: Autoimmune hepatitis has strict diagnostic criteria that often require liver biopsy for confirmation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why liver biopsy is important.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) requires contrast media.
items.reason: MRCP offers several advantages over ERCP, including no need for contrast media or ionizing radiation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because MRCP does not require contrast media; the reason correctly states one of its advantages.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · DIAGNOSIS OF LIVER DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.assertion: Liver biopsy is essential for guiding management in alcoholic and nonalcoholic steatohepatitis.
items.reason: Aminotransferase levels are unreliable in reflecting severity in these conditions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why liver biopsy is essential.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE

items.subtopic: GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
items.assertion: Noninvasive tests can reliably separate early from advanced liver disease.
items.reason: These models are not reliable enough for regular use or repeated measures.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because noninvasive tests are not reliable enough to consistently separate early from advanced liver disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Liver Disease · GRADING AND STAGING OF LIVER DISEASE
